1: hallĕlûyāh ʾašrê-ʾîš yārēʾ ʾet-yhwh — Ps 112 opens with the same hallĕlûyāh as Ps 111, then immediately echoes Ps 111's closing formula: "blessed is the man who fears the LORD" mirrors "the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom" (Psalms 111:10). The two psalms are a diptych: Ps 111 describes YHWH's character; Ps 112 maps those same attributes onto the righteous person who fears him. bĕmiṣwōtāyw ḥāpēṣ mĕʾōd — "who greatly delights in his commandments" — ḥāpēṣ (to take pleasure in) is the same word as Psalms 111:2 ("those who find pleasure in them"). The acrostic begins anew with aleph.
2: gibbôr bāʾāreṣ yihyeh zarʿô — "mighty/powerful in the land will be his seed" — gibbôr is the word for warrior, hero (used of Boaz in Ruth 2:1). Here it denotes the social standing and influence the righteous man's descendants will carry. dôr yĕšārîm yĕbōrāk — "the generation of the upright will be blessed" — yĕšārîm (the upright) = those aligned with covenant order.
3: hôn-wāʿōšer bĕbêtô — "wealth and riches are in his house" — the wisdom tradition presents material blessing as a regular outcome of covenant faithfulness, without denying that the righteous sometimes suffer. wĕṣidqātô ʿōmedet lāʿad — "and his righteousness stands forever" — verbatim repetition from Psalms 111:3 (there said of YHWH's work). The transfer is deliberate: the righteous person shares in a righteousness with the permanence of YHWH's own.
4: zāraḥ baḥōšek ʾôr layyĕšārîm — "there rises/shines in the darkness a light for the upright" — zāraḥ is the verb for sunrise; it images a dawn breaking on those sitting in darkness. ḥannûn wĕraḥûm wĕṣaddîq — in Psalms 111:4 these three attributes (gracious, compassionate, righteous) describe YHWH; here they describe the righteous person. The structural mirroring is the psalm's point: the one who fears YHWH takes on the divine character.
5: ṭôb-ʾîš ḥônēn ûmalweh — "it goes well with a man who shows favor and lends" — ḥānan (to show favor, be gracious) in the Qal is the human equivalent of what YHWH does in divine grace. The wisdom tradition consistently praises generosity: Psalms 37:26 says "he is always generous and lends." yĕkhalkēl dĕbārāyw bĕmišpāṭ — "he manages his affairs with justice/right judgment" — the righteous person's practical dealings are ordered by mišpāṭ.
6: kî-lĕʿôlām lōʾ yimmôṭ — "for to eternity he will not be shaken/moved" — māṭaṭ (to waver, totter) is the same verb used of cosmic shaking (Psalms 46:6); the righteous person has the stability that belongs to the foundations of the world. lĕzēkher ʿôlām yihyeh ṣaddîq — "in everlasting remembrance will be the righteous" — to be remembered is to have one's deeds still operating; to be forgotten is a form of death.
7: miššĕmûʿāh rāʿāh lōʾ yîrāʾ — "of evil report he will not be afraid" — the righteous person is not destabilized by catastrophic news because his foundation is not circumstantial. nākôn libbô bāṭûaḥ bayhwāh — "his heart is established/firm, trusting in the LORD" — nākôn (Niphal of kûn, to be established, fixed) describes a settled, prepared heart. bāṭûaḥ (trusting, secure) is the disposition that makes fearlessness possible.
8: sāmûk libbô lōʾ yîrāʾ — "his heart is supported/braced; he will not fear" — sāmak (to lean on, support, prop) — from the same root as Psalms 111:8. The heart propped up by God is the heart that will not fear. ʿad ʾăšer yirʾeh bĕṣārāyw — "until he sees (what happens to) his enemies" — the righteous person will outlast those arrayed against him.
9: pizzar nātan lāʾebyônîm — "he scattered, he gave to the poor" — pizzar (Piel of pāzar) means to scatter, disperse, spread abroad — a vivid image of open-handed, non-calculated giving. Paul quotes this verse in 2 Corinthians 9:9 when arguing for generous Christian giving: "He has distributed freely, he has given to the poor; his righteousness endures forever." Paul applies this OT description of the righteous person to the generous believer. ṣidqātô ʿōmedet lāʿad — the third occurrence of this clause across the two psalms (Psalms 111:3, Psalms 112:3, Psalms 112:9), now said of the generous giver.
10: rāšāʿ yirʾeh wĕkāʿas — "the wicked will see and be vexed/angry" — the final letter of the acrostic (taw) is in taʾăwat rĕšāʿîm (the desire of the wicked). šinnāyw yaḥărōq wĕnāmās — "he will gnash his teeth and melt away" — ḥārak (to grind teeth, used of hostility in Psalms 37:12) and māsas (to melt, dissolve). taʾăwat rĕšāʿîm tōʾbēd — "the desire of the wicked will perish" — the closing antithesis to v6's "everlasting remembrance": the wicked leave no lasting trace.
